title: Super revamp may invite higher risks
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 The Albanese government’s move to revamp the superannuation performance test could result in waves of capital flowing to riskier investments in a bid to boost retirement outcomes for millions of people.
Days after announcing a consultation with industry on the annual super performance test, the government on Tuesday confirmed plans to make changes to the controversial test as part of broader productivity reforms.
“The government will strengthen the superannuation performance test to help reduce unintended barriers to investment for Australia’s $4.5 trillion superannuation sector, supporting the efficient flow of capital across the economy to drive innovation and economic growth, while helping Australians achieve better retirement outcomes,” the federal budget papers said.
“Reforms will ensure the test remains fit for purpose and supports strong member outcomes.” Alongside a suite of reforms to improve flagging productivity, Tuesday’s budget pointed to arguments by some in the industry that the test, in its current form, discourages investment in certain sectors, including venture capital.
Venture capital investments are typically on the high-risk end of the investment spectrum and can deliver outsized returns, but with a delay.
The current performance test was brought in five years ago to weed out underperformers and initially was hailed a success. In its first year, the test revealed 1 million people had their money sitting in dud, underperforming funds.
Since then, the number of funds failing the test has dramatically fallen, in part due to funds moving to huge benchmarks and avoiding investments in assets that have a long lead-in time for returns and distort short-term performance.
This has become an increasingly fraught issue for the government as it looks to the super sector to help boost productivity and nation-building through investing in areas such as housing, energy, venture capital and climate assets.
“We don’t want the performance test to unintentionally discourage investment in some of the most important parts of our economy,” Jim Chalmers said ahead of the budget.
But the performance test is not the only factor holding funds back from tipping more member money in these asset classes.
Major funds have, for several years, warned the government that similar assets in regions such as North America and Europe offered better returns. Super funds are required to invest in assets they believe will deliver the best returns to members.
The government on Tuesday confirmed that any changes to the performance test would ensure it supported strong member outcomes.
As part of this, it is weighing whether to include in the test products that sit on super platforms, such as those offered by Macquarie, HUB24 and Netwealth.
The test currently covers default, MySuper options offered by industry and retail funds, and a small number of trustee-directed platform products.
Externally managed products, such as the thousands available on platforms, are not captured under the current rules. Neither are retirement products.
But these products, which include managed investment schemes, sat alongside the failed Shield and First Guardian funds and are now under greater scrutiny amid fears trustees have taken a light-touch approach in their due diligence, exposing members to unacceptable risks.
Shield and First Guardian were two managed investment schemes offered on platforms run by Macquarie, Equity Trustees, Netwealth and Diversa. Both were put into liquidation in the past 18 months and are under investigation by the Australian Securities & Investments Commission for allegedly misusing investor funds. All up, the 12,000 investors in the two funds lost $1bn in super savings.
While Macquarie and Netwealth opted to repay investors a total of $422m, Equity Trustees and Diversa are holding firm in their refusal to pay any money and are now being sued by ASIC.
As it considers whether to apply the performance test to a wider range of products, the government on Tuesday said it would hand ASIC more than $12m next year to strengthen governance requirements, supervision and enforcement of managed investment schemes.
The regulator will receive a further $4m out to 2030 to further improve the governance requirements of these schemes. The government is currently consulting on potential changes to managed investment schemes and trustee oversight in the wake of the Shield and First Guardian failures.
summary: The Albanese government plans to revamp the superannuation performance test to encourage investment in riskier assets, aiming to enhance retirement outcomes while addressing concerns over recent fund failures.
author: Cliona O’Dowd
heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: The government is consulting on changes to the superannuation performance test to reduce barriers to investment in sectors like venture capital.
The performance test, introduced five years ago, has led to a significant reduction in underperforming funds but may discourage investment in long-term assets.
In response to recent fund failures, the government is increasing funding for ASIC to improve governance and oversight of managed investment schemes.
